summary_sections.title: Lesson Overview
summary_sections.content_html:
Overview

This lesson covers the fundamental measures of center used to analyze and summarize data sets. You'll learn how to calculate and apply the mean, median, and mode, and understand when each is most appropriate.

summary_sections.title: Definitions and Concepts
summary_sections.content_html:
Key Definitions

What is the difference between a sequence and a series?

A sequence is an ordered list of numbers, while a series is the sum of the terms in a sequence.

What is the 'index variable' in sigma notation?

The index variable (often k, i, or n) is the variable written below Σ that counts through integer values from the lower limit to the upper limit.

What is a 'partial sum' of a series?

A partial sum Sₙ adds only the first n terms of a series, giving a finite result even if the full series is infinite.

What is the 'upper limit' in sigma notation Σₖ₌₁ⁿ?

The upper limit n, written above Σ, is the final value the index variable takes. Summation includes this value (inclusive).

A savings plan deposits $200 the first month, $220 the second, $240 the third, and so on. Using sigma notation, what is the total deposited over 12 months?

aₖ = 200 + 20(k−1) = 180 + 20k. Total = Σₖ₌₁¹² (180 + 20k) = 12(180) + 20·12(13)/2 = 2160 + 1560 = $3,720. Alternatively: a₁ = 200, a₁₂ = 420, S₁₂ = 12(200 + 420)/2 = 3,720.

summary_sections.title: Real-World Applications
summary_sections.content_html:
Real-World Applications

Example: A student reads 10 pages on day 1 and 2 more pages each subsequent day. Express the total pages read in 7 days using sigma notation and evaluate.

Total = Σₖ₌₁⁷ (8 + 2k) = Σₖ₌₁⁷ 8 + 2·Σₖ₌₁⁷ k = 56 + 2(28) = 56 + 56 = 112. (Day 1: 10, Day 7: 22, S₇ = 7(10+22)/2 = 112.)
